CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well-organized and presents its method (HYPERBAND) and experimental protocol clearly, with main contributions stated early and a logical intro→method→experiments→conclusion flow. Notation and algorithmic description are readable and the role of inputs R and η and the SUCCESSIVEHALVING mechanics are clearly explained; figures and empirical comparisons (CIFAR-10, MNIST variants, SVHN, baselines like SMAC/TPE/Spearmint/SuccessiveHalving) are effective and informative. My main presentation complaint is that the paper repeatedly invokes theoretical guarantees yet defers the detailed treatment to another work (Li et al. 2016), which weakens the self-containment and makes the claims feel under-supported in this manuscript. Overall writing quality is good, with minor clarity loss only where proofs and formal guarantees are omitted. | B(4): The paper is generally well organized with a clear intro → method → experiments → conclusion flow and states the main contributions early (HYPERBAND design and empirical speedups). Notation and presentation are clear enough for practical use, related work is thorough, and the writing quality is good. Minor issues remain: a full theoretical treatment is deferred and the dependence on user-chosen inputs (R, η) is discussed but could use clearer practical guidance, preventing a top score.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is well-organized and presents its method (HYPERBAND) and experimental protocol clearly, with main contributions stated early and a logical intro→method→experiments→conclusion flow. Notation and algorithmic description are readable and the role of inputs R and η and the SUCCESSIVEHALVING mechanics are clearly explained; figures and empirical comparisons (CIFAR-10, MNIST variants, SVHN, baselines like SMAC/TPE/Spearmint/SuccessiveHalving) are effective and informative. My main presentation complaint is that the paper repeatedly invokes theoretical guarantees yet defers the detailed treatment to another work (Li et al. 2016), which weakens the self-containment and makes the claims feel under-supported in this manuscript. Overall writing quality is good, with minor clarity loss only where proofs and formal guarantees are omitted.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is generally well organized with a clear intro → method → experiments → conclusion flow and states the main contributions early (HYPERBAND design and empirical speedups). Notation and presentation are clear enough for practical use, related work is thorough, and the writing quality is good. Minor issues remain: a full theoretical treatment is deferred and the dependence on user-chosen inputs (R, η) is discussed but could use clearer practical guidance, preventing a top score.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): HYPERBAND proposes a concrete, general-purpose procedure that systematically nests SUCCESSIVEHALVING runs across multiple n–r tradeoffs to hedge exploration versus per-configuration resource allocation, which is a distinctive operational idea beyond plain early stopping or single-run SuccessiveHalving. The paper clearly differentiates itself from prior model-based optimizers (SMAC, TPE, Spearmint) and from pure SuccessiveHalving by the multi-bracket strategy and practical empirical gains. However, the core building blocks are combinations of existing techniques (SuccessiveHalving and multi-scale resource allocation) and the theoretical treatment is deferred, so while the engineering/perspective shift is notable it is not a radical theoretical advance — hence a notable but not maximal originality score. | B(4): HYPERBAND presents a notable, practical new strategy by combining multi-bracket runs of SUCCESSIVEHALVING to adaptively hedge the n–r tradeoff, which is a clear methodological innovation beyond straightforward early-stopping or single-run successive halving. The paper frames a familiar hyperparameter tuning problem but contributes a new, assumption-light bandit-based allocation perspective and shows strong empirical speedups across realistic tasks (CIFAR-10, SVHN, MNIST) compared to SMAC/TPE/Spearmint and random baselines. The technique is more than a trivial combination of prior pieces and is clearly differentiated from related work, though it stops short of a full theoretical treatment in this paper, so I view it as notable novelty rather than a radically new paradigm.

reviewer_a.justification: HYPERBAND proposes a concrete, general-purpose procedure that systematically nests SUCCESSIVEHALVING runs across multiple n–r tradeoffs to hedge exploration versus per-configuration resource allocation, which is a distinctive operational idea beyond plain early stopping or single-run SuccessiveHalving. The paper clearly differentiates itself from prior model-based optimizers (SMAC, TPE, Spearmint) and from pure SuccessiveHalving by the multi-bracket strategy and practical empirical gains. However, the core building blocks are combinations of existing techniques (SuccessiveHalving and multi-scale resource allocation) and the theoretical treatment is deferred, so while the engineering/perspective shift is notable it is not a radical theoretical advance — hence a notable but not maximal originality score.

reviewer_b.justification: HYPERBAND presents a notable, practical new strategy by combining multi-bracket runs of SUCCESSIVEHALVING to adaptively hedge the n–r tradeoff, which is a clear methodological innovation beyond straightforward early-stopping or single-run successive halving. The paper frames a familiar hyperparameter tuning problem but contributes a new, assumption-light bandit-based allocation perspective and shows strong empirical speedups across realistic tasks (CIFAR-10, SVHN, MNIST) compared to SMAC/TPE/Spearmint and random baselines. The technique is more than a trivial combination of prior pieces and is clearly differentiated from related work, though it stops short of a full theoretical treatment in this paper, so I view it as notable novelty rather than a radically new paradigm.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the empirical claims are well-motivated and supported by extensive experiments against strong baselines, but the manuscript defers the formal theoretical guarantees and omits some reproducibility details (e.g., statistical testing and code release). Because key worst-case guarantees and full mathematical derivations are not provided here and some empirical exceptions exist, a modest deduction is warranted. Thus I assign a mid-level soundness/correctness score reflecting solid empirical support but incomplete theoretical substantiation.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper clearly states its main empirical claims and presents extensive experiments against reasonable baselines (SMAC, TPE, Spearmint, random search, SUCCESSIVEHALVING, etc.), so empirical claims are partially supported. However, the promised theoretical guarantees are not provided in this paper (the detailed theory is deferred), so mathematical derivations/proofs are incomplete and key worst-case guarantees are not shown here. Experimental methodology appears broadly sound (multiple datasets and baselines) but the summary does not report statistical testing or code release, leaving some reproducibility and rigor questions. Overall the conclusions about empirical speedups are supported, but the theoretical claims and formal assumptions are insufficiently substantiated in this manuscript.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper clearly states its main claims and provides substantial empirical evidence across multiple datasets and strong baselines (SMAC, TPE, Spearmint, random, SUCCESSIVEHALVING), demonstrating large practical speedups in many cases. However, the formal theoretical treatment is deferred and mathematical guarantees are referenced but not fully derived here, and the authors acknowledge overhead and sensitivity to R and η; moreover, some baselines with early stopping can outperform HYPERBAND on certain tasks (e.g., SVHN). Overall the experimental methodology appears sound and conclusions are largely supported, but the lack of complete proofs and some empirical exceptions justify a minor deduction.

main_claims: Introduce HYPERBAND, a bandit-based, early-stopping configuration evaluation method that adaptively allocates resources and evaluates many more hyperparameter configurations than uniform allocation.

HYPERBAND is a general-purpose technique making minimal assumptions and combines multiple runs of SUCCESSIVEHALVING over different n–r tradeoffs to hedge the exploration vs per-configuration resource allocation problem.

Empirically, HYPERBAND is often orders of magnitude faster than random search and Bayesian optimization baselines on a range of tasks (iterations, data subsampling, feature subsampling), while having theoretical guarantees that its budget is only logarithmically larger than optimal SUCCESSIVEHALVING (detailed theory deferred).
_summary.methodology: HYPERBAND runs multiple brackets that call SUCCESSIVEHALVING with different choices of number of configurations n and minimum resource r for a fixed total budget B, using inputs R (max resource per config) and η to control elimination; it selects configurations by repeatedly allocating resources, evaluating intermediate validation loss, and halving (or 1/η-ing) the pool.
limitations: A single execution of HYPERBAND does approximately smax+1 times more work than running SUCCESSIVEHALVING once (cost overhead).
Choice of inputs R and η is required (R: max resource per configuration; η controls elimination); HYPERBAND’s performance depends on these choices.
A thorough theoretical treatment of HYPERBAND is deferred to Li et al. (2016) (not provided in this paper).
In some cases (e.g., SVHN) configuration-selection methods with early stopping (SMAC early) can outperform HYPERBAND.
_summary.topic_area: Hyperparameter optimization / automated machine learning (bandit-based configuration evaluation)

reviewer_a.name: Theory Purist
reviewer_a.background: Senior researcher in learning theory and optimization with a PhD in theoretical computer science; long experience proving lower/upper bounds for sequential decision problems.
traits: penalises papers that state theoretical guarantees but defer detailed proofs or present only informal proof sketches
requires clear formal statements of assumptions and worst-case bounds (will deduct points if assumptions are vague or unrealistic)
discounts empirical results that are not accompanied by matching theoretical insight or characterization of when/why the method works
expects at least one comparison to provably optimal baselines or lower bounds when claiming near-optimality
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Prioritizes mathematical rigor and completeness; will score highly only if formal guarantees are tight, fully proved, and assumptions are explicit.

reviewer_b.name: Practical AutoML Enthusiast
reviewer_b.background: Applied machine learning researcher/engineer focusing on hyperparameter optimization and AutoML systems, with experience deploying scalable tuning methods in production.
traits: rewards methods that produce large empirical speedups or resource savings across multiple realistic tasks (will boost score for consistent order-of-magnitude improvements)
prefers simple, assumption-light algorithms that are easy to implement and scale (will penalise approaches requiring complex model-specific machinery)
values broad empirical evaluation over many baselines and settings; expects comparisons to strong practical baselines like random search, Bayesian optimization, and multi-fidelity methods
gives credit for techniques that are robust and general-purpose even if formal theory is partial or deferred
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Focuses on practical impact and empirical robustness; will score highly for methods that are simple, scalable, and demonstrate substantial empirical benefits in realistic settings.
